name: Lilin
description: The Lilin are female demons in Jewish folklore, often associated with the night and danger to newborns and pregnant women. They are considered offspring or descendants of Lilith, the first wife of Adam in some traditions, who left him and refused to submit to his authority. Lilin are often depicted as seductive and dangerous, luring men astray and causing misfortune. Their origins can be traced back to Mesopotamian demonology, where similar female spirits were believed to roam the night, posing threats to humans. Over time, the Lilin evolved within Jewish tradition, becoming symbols of female power, rebellion, and the darker aspects of sexuality. They are believed to be numerous and capable of shapeshifting, making them difficult to identify and defend against. Protection amulets and incantations were often used to ward off their influence, particularly during vulnerable times such as childbirth.
subtitle: Female demons of Jewish folklore, descendants of Lilith.
appearance: Lilin are often described as beautiful, seductive women with long, flowing hair and piercing eyes. They may also possess wings or other demonic features, depending on the specific depiction. Their appearance is often intended to be alluring and deceptive, masking their true malevolent nature.
habitat: Lilin are believed to inhabit desolate places, such as ruins, graveyards, and forests. They are most active during the night, particularly in places where people are vulnerable, such as bedrooms and birthing chambers.
behavior: Lilin are generally depicted as malicious and predatory. They seek to harm humans, particularly men and newborns. They use their seductive powers to lure men into sexual encounters, which can result in illness, madness, or even death. They also target pregnant women and infants, seeking to steal their vitality or cause miscarriages and infant mortality.

keyMyths.title: Lilith and the First Night
keyMyths.description: This myth recounts Lilith's departure from Adam and her subsequent transformation into a demon. Refusing to submit to Adam's dominance, she flew away and consorted with demons, birthing countless Lilin. This story highlights the Lilin's connection to Lilith's rebellion and their inherent defiance of patriarchal norms. Lilith's flight is the origin of all Lilin.

keyMyths.title: Lilin and Childbirth
keyMyths.description: Lilin are particularly dangerous during childbirth, as they seek to harm the mother and newborn. Amulets and incantations are often used to protect against their influence. This fear stems from the Lilin's perceived envy of human mothers and their desire to steal their offspring. Many traditions arose to combat this threat.

keyMyths.title: Encounters with Lilin in Dreams
keyMyths.description: Some stories tell of men encountering Lilin in their dreams, where they appear as seductive figures. These encounters often result in nightmares, illness, or even death. These tales serve as warnings against succumbing to temptation and the dangers of the night. These dreams are said to be incredibly realistic and disturbing.

sources.author: Raphael Patai
sources.description: Explores the role of female deities and demonic figures in Jewish tradition, including Lilith and the Lilin.
sources.text: Explores the role of female deities and demonic figures in Jewish tradition, including Lilith and the Lilin.

sources.description: While not directly about Lilin, it discusses the nature of demons and their role in Jewish thought.
